transcript.clean_text: Sunshine, once again, stage seven of the Vuelta, destination Oliva. And of course, time to just gather what had happened the day prior. Sepp Kuss, of course, taking the stage, but Lenny Martinez leading. This was to be about the quick men, however, and Kaden Groves started the day as out and out favorite, already having won back-to-back sprints. How would this one pan out? Well, Sepp Kuss had taken victory on stage six, but the red jersey belonging to Lenny Martinez, and certainly one he'd like to protect going into the high ground of the coming weekend, but had to deal with a sprint day in the meantime. Well, nothing too complicated, you might think, but once we hit the coast, wind was to be a factor, and certainly after the intermediate sprint, the run for home, some twisty technical turns later on would have an impact. As it was, with such a distance of 201 km, nobody really fancied being in the break, especially as we'd had a 40-rider group go off up the road on day one. Off the road went Geraint Thomas, unfortunately. Quick visit to a cornfield. Well, he was to be back at the medical car. They checked his knee out, and his demeanor was not at its best. Jose Herrada of Cofidis, and then finally Ander Okamika of Burgos BH were duly caught. Nothing much happened until we started to get down towards the coast. High cloud saw off the shadows, and some of the technical turns saw off quite a few challenges. There were crashes, and some more significant than others, some sad to tell you. More riders departing the race, but 35 km out was an intermediate sprint, and Kaden Groves duly took it. Challenging him, Marijn van den Berg, and Jonas Vingegaard getting up for some bonus seconds. As you can see the peloton almost as wide as it was long at this point. So, not much room to maneuver and if you did get it wrong then the consequences could be severe. Lots of riders finding themselves on the deck but not as serious as the last running. Thymen Arensman with just 5 km to go down heavy crashing into another rider who'd gone down and unfortunately for Ineos Grenadiers he's out of the race. Then the run down to this last corner, a significant one as well. Once it unwound there would only be 350 m remaining. Everyone knew they need to be in a good position. Gonzales dive bombed for Kiko Real that sent a few skittering not to the ambitions though of Orluis Aular, the Venezuelan champion spearing up through the middle but Geoffrey Soupe usually in support of others notably this time he was supposed to be leading out Dries Van Gestel. Van Gestel wasn't there so Soupe helped himself to the main course. Brilliant. Two great names of sprinting but only for those in the know. Geoffrey Soupe a support rider who used to work so hard for Nacer Bouhanni today he got his biggest win of his career. Just finishing ahead of Aular by a third of a wheel in it. Ed Turns clever rider making up the base podium ahead of Milano. Kaden Groves disappointed but adds to his points tally overall. And our leader Lenny Martinez going into the mountains this weekend. He only has an 8-second advantage over Sep Kuss, Marc Soler, Wilco Kelderman and Sepp Strake. Vingegaard into the top 10 courtesy of the bonus seconds he picked up today out at the intermediate sprint. The points jersey however stays on the shoulders of Kaden Groves who put a disappointment of not winning a a third stage in this welter behind him. He leads this competition by a handsome margin from Vendrame and now Geoffrey Soupe vaults into third place. La Vuelta We race on and tomorrow it could be described as hell. Certainly will be if the weather comes in. Storm warnings are over Spain. Whether we'll get away with it, we should wait and see. The final climb the Catí is a downhill run off the peak and then a flat dive to the finish. It could prove dramatic. Well, every day has done so far, so why should it not be?
